burial.plot_notes: Lake View Cemetery Seattle WA. Shared stone with wife: "WALTER OAKES 1864-1911 and his wife MARY BEEKMAN TAYLOR 1866-1948."

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Fletcher Oakes Sr. — 9th president of Northern Pacific Railroad Company, 1888-1893. Educated by private tutors and Boston's Eliot School. Very wealthy and influential; spent summers in Burlington ME as a child. Walter's grandfather Jonathan was from Burlington. SOURCE: The History of Burlington, Maine, by Alan Hawkins.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of the NP Railroad president
narrative_blocks.body: Walter Oakes was the son of Thomas Fletcher Oakes Sr., president of the Northern Pacific Railroad from 1888 to 1893. After graduating Harvard in 1887, Walter moved to Tacoma and then Seattle, where he co-founded the Puget Sound & Alaska Steamship Company with Charles E. Peabody — which later became the Alaska Steamship Company.

With Charles E. Peabody he co-founded the Puget Sound & Alaska Steamship Company and ran the Roslyn Fuel Company as president — the boats and the coal of the Klondike-era Sound. He led Seattle’s Harvard Club and was dead at forty-six.

parents.father_research_notes: X02139 Walter Oakes (1864-1911) — co-founder Alaska Steamship Company; Harvard '87; son of Thomas F. Oakes Sr., president of Northern Pacific Railroad.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Beekman Taylor (b.1866 NY, d.1948 Seattle). Buried Lake View Cemetery Seattle with Walter.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson of the Northern Pacific Railway's president
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Thomas Fletcher Oakes (1843-1919) entered railway service in 1863 and rose to the presidency of the Northern Pacific from 1888 to 1893, after starting on the Kansas Pacific. The line that he ran from the Great Lakes to Puget Sound was central to the Pacific Northwest's transformation from frontier to settled commerce.

burial.plot_notes: Fairview Cemetery New Britain CT. Section 14, 2055, west tier, south of center.

sources.label: The New York Times obituary, December 1942

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Co-founder Alaska Steamship Company

narrative_blocks.body: Jeanne Stanley married Thomas Fletcher Oakes in 1936 in Bloomfield, Connecticut, but the marriage ended in divorce by 1940. She moved alone to New York City, living at an apartment on Madison Avenue. She was active in Hartford civic life — the Hartford Junior League, the 1938 Easter Seals committee, and the 1941 Greek War Relief collection drive.

burialCemetery.name: Lake View Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Seattle, King County WA. Burial site of X02139 Walter Oakes and Mary Beekman Taylor Oakes. Inscription: "WALTER OAKES 1864-1911 and his wife MARY BEEKMAN TAYLOR 1866-1948."

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.

INST057.name: Phillips Academy Andover
INST057.notes:
HD3067 (Charles Phelps Taft), HD3063 (Edward Swift Isham Jr.), and X02345 (Samuel Finley Brown Morse) attended Phillips Academy Andover. STAGE 6Y-174: X02848 John Adams was principal of Phillips Academy Andover for 22 years (1810-1832) — the longest principalship of the 19th century. Found Academy at 23 pupils after interregnum; grew enrollment to 100 by 1817 and held that level for 15 years. Salary $900 + house. Resigned at 60 saying no man over 60 ought to teach. Holmes referenced him in 'The School Boy' at the 1878 centennial. X02847 Rev. John Ripley Adams (Yale 1821, John Adams's son) taught at Phillips Academy briefly after Yale before entering Andover Theological Seminary 1826.
[former/alt detail] Phillips Academy, Andover MA (1778)
